count Rennenberg (c. 1550 – 23 July 1581), was [stadtholder](/wiki/Stadtholder "Stadtholder") of [Friesland](/wiki/Friesland "Friesland"), [Groningen](/wiki/Groningen_(province) "Groningen (province)"), [Drenthe](/wiki/Drenthe "Drenthe") and [Overijssel](/wiki/Overijssel "Overijssel") in the service of [Philip II of Spain](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain") from 1577 to 1581. The Lalaing family came from [Hainaut](/wiki/Hainaut_(province) "Hainaut (province)") and had a tradition of governing. His father was [Philip de Lalaing](/wiki/Philip_de_Lalaing,_2nd_Count_of_Hoogstraten "Philip de Lalaing, 2nd Count of Hoogstraten"), count of Hoogstraten; his mother, Anna of Rennenberg.

Ros, Fokko Ubertus (1964). [*Rennenberg en de Groningse malcontenten*](https://books.google.com/books?id=_pATAQAAIAAJ) (in Dutch). Van Gorkum, H.J. Prakke & H.M. Prakke. p. 18. Retrieved 25 January 2022.
Erik Swart, Lalaing, Cornelia van, in: Digitaal Vrouwenlexicon van Nederland. URL: <http://resources.huygens.knaw.nl/vrouwenlexicon/lemmata/data/Lalaing> [13/01/2014]

Stadtholder of Philip II of Spain, 1577–1581

**George de Lalaing** count Rennenberg (c. 1550 – 23 July 1581), was [stadtholder](/wiki/Stadtholder "Stadtholder") of [Friesland](/wiki/Friesland "Friesland"), [Groningen](/wiki/Groningen_(province) "Groningen (province)"), [Drenthe](/wiki/Drenthe "Drenthe") and [Overijssel](/wiki/Overijssel "Overijssel") in the service of [Philip II of Spain](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain") from 1577 to 1581. The Lalaing family came from [Hainaut](/wiki/Hainaut_(province) "Hainaut (province)") and had a tradition of governing. His father was [Philip de Lalaing](/wiki/Philip_de_Lalaing,_2nd_Count_of_Hoogstraten "Philip de Lalaing, 2nd Count of Hoogstraten"), count of Hoogstraten; his mother, Anna of Rennenberg.

## Life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=George_de_Lalaing,_Count_of_Rennenberg&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Life")]

He was born around 1550, probably in [Hoogstraten Castle (Gelmelslot)](/wiki/Hoogstraten_Castle_(Gelmelslot) "Hoogstraten Castle (Gelmelslot)") in the [Campine](/wiki/Campine "Campine").[[1]](#cite_note-1) Rennenberg (as he was known in the Netherlands) was appointed stadtholder of the Northern provinces by the [States General of the Netherlands](/wiki/States_General_of_the_Netherlands "States General of the Netherlands") after the [Pacification of Ghent](/wiki/Pacification_of_Ghent "Pacification of Ghent") in 1577, on a proposal from [William of Orange](/wiki/William_the_Silent "William the Silent"). He allowed a number of important reforms in Friesland, such as the introduction of the *Gedeputeerde Staten* (Delegated States), and the forming of a fourth quarter in the [States of Friesland](/wiki/States_of_Friesland "States of Friesland") to represent the eleven Frisian cities. He was a firm believer in the Pacification of Ghent as a means to reconcile the rebellious Calvinists with their Catholic king. When the treaty resulted in Protestantism gaining ground, notably in [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"), [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent") and [Bruges](/wiki/Bruges "Bruges"), he abandoned the cause of the rebels for the king on 3 March 1580, with the support of the city of Groningen, according to tradition convinced by his sister [Cornelia van Lalaing](/wiki/Cornelia_van_Lalaing "Cornelia van Lalaing").[[2]](#cite_note-2) The [rest of the province](/wiki/Ommelanden "Ommelanden") remained loyal to the rebel cause. He attempted to [besiege Steenwijk](/wiki/Siege_of_Steenwijk_(1580%E2%80%931581) "Siege of Steenwijk (1580–1581)") but had to raise the siege once [John Norreys](/wiki/John_Norris_(soldier) "John Norris (soldier)") arrived and relieved the place. He was then further defeated by Norreys [at Kollum](/wiki/Battle_of_Kollum "Battle of Kollum"). Rennenberg, already ill, died at [Groningen](/wiki/Groningen_(city) "Groningen (city)") on 23 July 1581 and was buried in the Groningen [Martinikerk](/wiki/Martinikerk_(Groningen) "Martinikerk (Groningen)"). The city remained the scene of military action until 1594. Rennenberg's defection polarized the Dutch population further along religious lines. In the rebel provinces, Catholics would no longer be trusted with high posts.
